title: Psalms 15
subtitle: Who May Dwell in Your Sanctuary?

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Who May Live on Your Holy Mountain?”

heb.gloss: to sojourn / to dwell as guest
heb.paragraph: "LORD, who may dwell (gūr) in your sacred tent? Who may live on your holy mountain?" (v.1). The entrance question: who qualifies for God’s presence? The answer is ethical, not ritual: blameless conduct, righteous action, truthful speech, no slander, no wrong to neighbour.

refs.note: "Who may ascend the mountain of the LORD?" — the companion entrance liturgy.
"Who can dwell with the consuming fire?" — the prophetic version.

notes.note: "Who may dwell in your sacred tent?" The question assumes not everyone qualifies. Access to God requires character.
"Whose walk is blameless, who does what is righteous, who speaks the truth from their heart." Three categories: conduct, action, speech. All internal → external.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the "sacred tent" and "holy mountain" are Zion’s temple. The question is both literal (who enters the sanctuary?) and theological (who lives in God’s presence?). Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: the ethical requirements show that faith and morality are inseparable. Calvin: you cannot worship God on Sunday and wrong your neighbour on Monday. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: gūr means to sojourn as a guest. Dwelling with God is guest-status, not ownership. We are guests in God’s house, admitted by his grace and expected to honour his rules. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the entrance question was asked at the temple gate. The priests answered with the ethical requirements. This psalm preserves that liturgy. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the entrance requirements are entirely moral, not ceremonial. No mention of sacrifice, purity rituals, or priestly requirements. Character is the admission ticket. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "speaks the truth from their heart" — not just accurate speech but sincere speech. The heart and the tongue are aligned. No double heart (cf. Ps 12:2). Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: A Torah entrance liturgy — the worshipper asks at the gate: who may enter? The answer (vv.2–5): the one whose walk is blameless, who does right, speaks truth, does no wrong to a neighbour, keeps oaths even when it hurts, and does not accept bribes. Character, not credentials.

sections.header: Verses 4–5 — “The One Who Does These Things Will Never Be Shaken”

heb.gloss: will never be shaken / will not be moved
heb.paragraph: "Whoever does these things will never be shaken (lōʾ yimmōṭ)" (v.5). The psalm’s reward: stability. The person of integrity has a foundation that does not collapse. This connects to Psalm 1’s tree and Psalm 11’s foundations — the righteous are rooted, unshakable, permanent.

refs.note: "The wise man built his house on the rock... it did not fall" — the same unshakable-foundation promise.
"The righteous will never be shaken" — the identical promise.

notes.note: "Who keeps an oath even when it hurts." Integrity means fulfilling commitments when the cost is high, not just when it is convenient. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

"Whoever does these things will never be shaken." The one-sentence promise. Character produces stability. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "keeps an oath even to their own hurt" is the most demanding requirement. It distinguishes genuine integrity from convenient morality. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.

Calvin: "never shaken" is conditional on character, but the character itself is a gift of grace. Calvin: we do not earn stability; we receive the grace that produces the character that results in stability. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "swears to their own hurt" means fulfilling a vow even when circumstances change and the vow becomes costly. Integrity is tested in the gap between promise and fulfilment.
The NET: lōʾ yimmōṭ (not be moved/shaken) uses the mot root — the same root in Psalm 10:6. The righteous share the REAL stability the wicked only imagined.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: "never shaken" is the psalm’s payoff: not prosperity or fame but stability. The person of integrity stands when everything around them falls. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the requirements escalate: keeping costly oaths, refusing profitable bribes. Integrity is tested by what you give up, not what you gain. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "never be shaken" echoes the wicked’s boast in 10:6 ("I will never be shaken"). The difference: the wicked’s confidence is self-generated; the righteous person’s is God-grounded. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: More requirements: despise the vile, honour the God-fearing, keep oaths even at cost, lend without usury, refuse bribes against the innocent (vv.4–5a). The promise: "whoever does these things will never be shaken" (v.5b). The psalm is 5 verses of character and one sentence of reward. The emphasis is clear: the life matters; the stability follows.

rec.text: ""Who may dwell in your sacred tent?" The question assumes not everyone qualifies. Access to God requires character."

rows.label: The Promise — 15:4–5
rows.text: Costly oaths kept, bribes refused. "Whoever does these things will never be shaken."

themes.note: Holiness peaks: entrance requirements are ethical, not ritual. Worship: the temple-gate liturgy. Justice: costly integrity.

prayer_prompt: As you consider the question 'LORD, who may dwell in your sanctuary?' consider asking God whether your daily conduct — your speech, your business dealings, your promises — would qualify you as a guest in His presence.
